original_abstract: Supervised machine learning traditionally involves classifying novel data points based on a foundation of pre-labeled training examples. In this work, we demonstrate that the support vector machine—a powerful and widely used binary classifier—can be implemented on a quantum architecture with a complexity that scales logarithmically with both the vector dimensionality and the size of the training set. This represents an exponential speedup over classical sampling algorithms that require polynomial time. The cornerstone of this quantum "big data" approach is a novel nonsparse matrix exponentiation technique, which enables the efficient inversion of the training data’s inner-product (kernel) matrix.
humanized_abstract: Classifying new, unseen data is a central theme in supervised machine learning (ML) where the classification is learned from a set of labeled training samples. Here we show that we can classify new samples using a support vector machine (SVM) implemented on a quantum processor while the number of parameters required scales only logarithmically with the dimension of the samples and the number of samples in the training set, i.e., exponentially faster than the polynomial time required to classify samples classically using sampling algorithms. A key component of this "big data" quantum ML method is a nonsparse matrix exponentiation method that allows the efficient computation of the inverse of the inner-product (kernel) data matrix.
